zh:
波利尼西亚神话铺展在地球上最大的一片水域：以夏威夷、拉帕努伊（复活节岛）与新西兰奥特亚罗瓦为三角顶点，中间散落着塔希提、萨摩亚、汤加、马克萨斯与库克群岛。乘双体独木舟看星辨浪的航海者把同一套神名带向四方，于是同一位神在不同群岛长成了不同模样——坦加罗阿在新西兰是海神，在塔希提是从壳中破出的创世者塔阿罗阿；毛伊在每个群岛都钓过岛、捕过太阳。诸神与人共享一部谱系，酋长的家世可以一代代上数到天父地母，念诵谱系本身就是仪式。

这套传统没有本族书写的古代文本，全靠咏唱、谱系与祭司学校口耳相传，十九世纪起才被大规模笔录：乔治·格雷1855年的《波利尼西亚神话》保存了毛利学者特朗吉卡赫克笔下的创世与毛伊全篇；夏威夷的创世咏唱《库姆利波》长两千余行，1889年由卡拉卡瓦国王刊印，1897年经利留卡拉尼女王译成英文；特乌伊拉·亨利1928年的《古塔希提》整理了传教士奥斯蒙德收集的塔希提祭仪与咏唱；戴维·马洛、福南德与贝克威思则为夏威夷留下了成体系的记录。支配这个世界的两个观念是玛纳（人与物中流转的效力）与塔普（神圣不可触碰的禁制），神庙、酋长、渔场与一株待伐的树都被它们框定。

它的气质是航海者的：世界是从海里钓起、从壳里凿出的，祖先的故土哈瓦基既在来路上也在亡灵的归途上。这也是一套仍在呼吸的传统——基拉韦厄火口边至今有人向佩蕾献花，新西兰的公路曾为一条塔尼法改线，远航独木舟霍库莱阿号仍循着毛伊鱼钩形状的星列横渡太平洋。
en:
Polynesian mythology is spread across the largest expanse of water on earth, a triangle cornered by Hawaii, Rapa Nui and Aotearoa New Zealand, with Tahiti, Samoa, Tonga, the Marquesas and the Cook Islands scattered between. Voyagers steering double-hulled canoes by stars and swells carried one set of divine names in every direction, and the same god grew a different shape on each archipelago: Tangaroa is the sea god in New Zealand and, as Taaroa, the shell-cracking creator in Tahiti; Maui fished up islands and snared the sun wherever the canoes landed. Gods and people share a single genealogy — a chief's line could be recited generation by generation back to the sky father and earth mother, and the recitation was itself a rite.

The tradition produced no ancient native texts; it lived in chant, genealogy and the priestly schools until the great transcriptions of the nineteenth century. George Grey's Polynesian Mythology (1855) preserves the creation cycle and the Maui saga from the pen of the Maori scholar Te Rangikaheke; the Hawaiian creation chant Kumulipo, over two thousand lines long, was printed by King Kalakaua in 1889 and translated by Queen Liliuokalani in 1897; Teuira Henry's Ancient Tahiti (1928) assembled the chants and rituals collected by the missionary John Orsmond; David Malo, Abraham Fornander and Martha Beckwith did the same for Hawaii. Two ideas govern the whole world: mana, the potency that flows through persons and things, and tapu, the sacred prohibition — together they framed temples, chiefs, fishing grounds and any tree about to be felled.

Its temper is a navigator's. The world is something fished from the sea or chiselled out of a shell, and the ancestral homeland Hawaiki lies both on the route the canoes came by and on the road the dead travel back. It is also a tradition still breathing: flowers are laid for Pele at the rim of Kilauea, a New Zealand highway was rerouted around a taniwha, and the voyaging canoe Hokulea still crosses the Pacific by the hook-shaped stars of Maui.
